title: Chapter 22: Blood
content: Chloe's father had never visited her at Serenity.

Not once. Not during the fourteen days she'd been locked in Room 214. Not during the medication rounds, the forced olanzapine, the solitary confinement with the deadbolt Holloway had drilled into her door. Her mother had delivered her — handcuffed in the back of the Tesla — and then disappeared back down the gravel drive before Chloe could even look back. Her father hadn't even done that. He'd signed the commitment papers from his office in Menlo Park, between a board meeting and a venture call, and then gone back to extracting value from human misery and calling it disruption.
Now he'd donated $250,000 to an organization whose stated purpose was protecting the facilities that made daughters like Chloe disappear.
Chloe stared at the spreadsheet for a long time.

Layla, beside her, was quiet. She understood — Layla's own father had committed her for refusing to build surveillance software. She knew what it felt like to see a parent's name on a document that proved they'd paid to erase you.
"You don't have to be the one," Layla said. "We can handle this. Anna can publish the donor list. Maria can investigate. You don't have to confront him personally."
Chloe was quiet for another moment. Then she closed the tablet.
"Yes I do."
She called him from the Boulder Airbnb, on speakerphone, with five women watching.

He answered on the third ring. "Chloe." Not *hi.* Not *are you okay.* Not *I saw the news and I've been worried.* Just her name, flat and neutral, like she was a business call he hadn't been expecting.
"You donated a quarter of a million dollars to the Family Wellness Alliance," she said. "Three days ago."
A pause. The kind of pause that meant he was calculating, not feeling.
"Where did you hear that?"
"From their donor records. Which we accessed. Because their cybersecurity is as good as Serenity's was."
Another pause. Longer this time.
"I donated to an organization that supports families who've been unfairly targeted," he said. "It has nothing to do with you."

"It has everything to do with me. The organization you donated to is run by Patricia Holloway — the woman who prescribed me olanzapine for having opinions. The organization is designed to protect facilities exactly like the one you and Mom paid to lock me in. You're funding the people who drugged me."
"I funded a legal defense organization. What they do with the money is their business."
"That's," Chloe said, "the most venture capitalist thing you've ever said. And you once told me that Uber's labor violations were 'a regulatory friction problem.'"
"I'm not going to argue with you, Chloe. You've made your choices. I've made mine."
"You made a choice to lock me up. You made a choice to drug me. And now you've made a choice to fund the people who want to keep doing that to other women."
"I made a choice to protect my family from the consequences of your... activism."
Chloe laughed. It wasn't the weaponized laugh. It was the real one — sharp and bright and genuinely amused, because her father was, in this moment, the most predictable man on earth.
"Your family," she said. "You mean your reputation. Your board seat. Your limited partners who don't want to be associated with a VC whose daughter calls him an exploiter on TikTok."
"This conversation is over."

"It's not." Chloe's voice went cold — not the performative cold she used on Holloway, but the real kind. The kind that came from the part of her that had been cheek-pilling antidepressants for two weeks in a room with a camera that never blinked. "You have forty-eight hours to withdraw your donation from the Family Wellness Alliance and issue a public statement supporting our investigation. If you don't, your name goes on the donor list we publish. Next to Patricia Holloway's organization. Next to every facility we expose. The Verge. The Washington Post. Every outlet that ran the Serenity story. Everyone will know that Chloe Chen's father funded the people trying to keep women locked up."
"That's blackmail."
"That's accountability. There's a difference."
She hung up.
The room was silent.

Sophia spoke first. Her voice was quiet — the voice of someone who'd had a similar conversation with her own family and lost. "He's not going to withdraw. He's your father, but he's also a venture capitalist. Venture capitalists don't withdraw from positions they've already taken. They double down."
"I know," Chloe said.
"So you're going to publish his name."
"Yes."
"Even though he's your father."

Chloe looked at the phone. At the screen where her father's contact still read "Dad" — a label she'd set when she was sixteen and hadn't changed because changing it felt like admitting something she wasn't ready to admit.
"He stopped being my father," she said, "the day he signed the commitment papers. Everything after that is just... paperwork."
She changed the contact name. Not to his name. Not to "VC Dad" or "Father" or anything that acknowledged the relationship.
She changed it to: *Donor #1.*
Two days later, Chloe's father had not withdrawn his donation.

He'd issued a statement, through a PR firm, calling Chloe's allegations "the unfortunate consequences of a mental health crisis that began long before her hospitalization" and affirming his "continued support for families seeking legitimate medical care for their loved ones."
He'd doubled down.
Sophia had been right.
Chloe read the statement once. Twice. Then she forwarded it to Layla with one word:
*Publish.*
That night, the Women of Serenity published the Family Wellness Alliance donor list.
Forty-seven names. Forty-seven families. Two million dollars. Every donation amount. Every transfer date. Every facility those families had used. Every patient they'd committed.

Chloe's father was at the top of the list. $250,000. Transferred three days ago. Associated facility: Serenity Wellness Institute, San Mateo County, California. Associated patient: Chloe Chen, Patient 17-C.

The list went viral within hours. News outlets that had covered the Serenity story now had a second story: *The Network Behind the Facilities.* The families who'd paid to make their daughters disappear were no longer anonymous. They were named. They were cited. They were, as Rachel had predicted three weeks ago in a diner in Williamsburg, scared.
And Chloe, sitting on the balcony of the Boulder Airbnb, watched her phone light up with a call from Donor #1.
She let it ring.
Six times. Seven. The voicemail icon appeared.

She didn't listen to it. Didn't need to. She already knew what it said — what every voicemail from every parent who'd been exposed said. *How could you. After everything we did for you. You're sick. You need help.*
She deleted it without listening.

Behind her, through the sliding glass door, Jamie was laughing again. Maya was explaining shot put trajectories to Elena. Layla was building the next version of the archive. Rachel was on the phone with Maria Okonkwo, coordinating the legal response.

The organization was running. The machine was working. The map now had two red dots crossed out, nine more to go, and forty-seven named donors who'd just learned that anonymity was a luxury they'd lost.
Chloe looked at her phone. At the contact list where "Dad" had been replaced by "Donor #1." At the voicemail she'd deleted without hearing.
"Two down," she said, to no one, to the Flatirons, to the stars she still couldn't see from Brooklyn. "Nine to go."
And somewhere in her chest, a door that had been locked for a very long time finally swung open.
